blocks.text: Then Jesus asked him, “Do you see these great buildings? Not one stone will be left here upon another; all will be thrown down.”
2 : See Jer 26.6; Mic 3.12. Roman soldiers demolished the buildings in 70 , but massive stones of part of the western retaining wall of the Temple platform remain a religious site.
When he was sitting on the Mount of Olives opposite the temple, Peter, James, John, and Andrew asked him privately,
“Tell us, when will this be, and what will be the sign that all these things are about to be accomplished?”
4 : The disciples connect the Temple's destruction to the coming of the kingdom at the end of days.
Then Jesus began to say to them, “Beware that no one leads you astray.
Many will come in my name and say, ‘I am he!’ and they will lead many astray.
6 : , lit., “I am”; see 6.50n. Cf. 9.39, where Jesus approves of an exorcist using his “name.”
When you hear of wars and rumors of wars, do not be alarmed; this must take place, but the end is still to come.
For nation will rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom; there will be earthquakes in various places; there will be famines. This is but the beginning of the birth pangs.

8 : , a common apocalyptic metaphor for the human suffering associated with the last days (see Isa 13.6–8; Hos 13.13; Rom 8.22; 1 Thess 5.3). Other images in this verse echo passages that anticipate God's redemption (see, e.g., Isa 13.13; 14.30; 19.2; Jer 22.23; Rev 6.8; 11.13; 16.18).
“As for yourselves, beware; for they will hand you over to councils; and you will be beaten in synagogues; and you will stand before governors and kings because of me, as a testimony to them.
9 : , lit., “you yourselves,” designating both Jesus's companions (13.3) and Mark's audience (13.14). , see 9.31; 10.33. , Jewish governing bodies and gathering places in Judea and the wider world.
And the good news must first be proclaimed to all nations.

10 : , see 1.1n.; 1.14. , or “Gentiles,” used in Jewish literature to denote non-Jews, and in Roman propaganda of the Empire's conquered peoples. God's reign will be universal, eclipsing religious, ethnic, and political divisions.
When they bring you to trial and hand you over, do not worry beforehand about what you are to say; but say whatever is given you at that time, for it is not you who speak, but the Holy Spirit.
Brother will betray brother to death, and a father his child, and children will rise against parents and have them put to death;
12 : As in other apocalyptic literature (e.g., 2 Esd 6.24), allegiance to God's kingdom can prove divisive in the context of existing social structures (cf. 3.31–35; Jn 9.18–23; 16.2).
and you will be hated by all because of my name. But the one who endures to the end will be saved.
13 : The full establishment of God's kingdom means the of the present evil; see vv. 4,7,8. , the destiny of the faithful at judgment day; cf. 10.26n.
“But when you see the desolating sacrilege set up where it ought not to be (let the reader understand), then those in Judea must flee to the mountains;

14 : , allusion to the defilement of the Temple during the Maccabean Era (see Dan 7.27; 11.31; 12.11); may also refer to the Zealots’ occupation of the Temple during the Jewish War (66–70 CE). , addresses Mark's audience, perhaps to support those who fled the violence in Jerusalem.
the one on the housetop must not go down or enter the house to take anything away;
the one in the field must not turn back to get a coat.
Woe to those who are pregnant and to those who are nursing infants in those days!
Pray that it may not be in winter.
18 : , when adverse weather makes travel treacherous.
For in those days there will be suffering, such as has not been from the beginning of the creation that God created until now, no, and never will be.
19 : , the Greek term “thlipsis” is used for suffering that has cosmic implications; see vv. 24–25; Dan 12.1; 2 Cor 4.17; Rev 2.9–10; 7.14.
And if the Lord had not cut short those days, no one would be saved; but for the sake of the elect, whom he chose, he has cut short those days.

20 : A shorter timeframe curtails the suffering of the faithful. , or “chosen,” applied in the Hebrew Bible to Israel (see Isa 42.1; 43.20) and in the NT to the church (see Rom 8.33; Eph 1.4–5; 1 Pet 1.2; 2.9; cf. Rom 11.28–29).
And if anyone says to you at that time, ‘Look! Here is the Messiah!’ or ‘Look! There he is!’—do not believe it.
False messiahs and false prophets will appear and produce signs and omens, to lead astray, if possible, the elect.
21–22 : , see 1.1n.; 8.28; 13.5. Josephus mentions prophets who performed signs during the revolt ( 20.97,168–70).
But be alert; I have already told you everything.
“But in those days, after that suffering, the sun will be darkened, and the moon will not give its light,
and the stars will be falling from heaven, and the powers in the heavens will be shaken.
24–25 : See 15.33; Isa 13.10; 34.4; 50.3; Ezek 32.7–8; Joel 2.10,31; Am 8.9. , shorthand for the “day of the Lord,” when God's kingdom will prevail.
Then they will see ‘the Son of Man coming in clouds’ with great power and glory.
Then he will send out the angels, and gather his elect from the four winds, from the ends of the earth to the ends of heaven.

26–27 : , see 2.10n.; in Dan 7.13–14, this figure assumes a heavenly throne after the beasts have been deposed. . The “elect” comprise all who remain loyal to God's reign, regardless of nationality, ethnicity, or religious tradition; see v. 20n.
“From the fig tree learn its lesson: as soon as its branch becomes tender and puts forth its leaves, you know that summer is near.
So also, when you see these things taking place, you know that he is near, at the very gates.
Truly I tell you, this generation will not pass away until all these things have taken place.
30 : , see 8.12n. If literal, it means that God's kingdom will arrive within a few decades (cf. 9.1); if metaphorical, it could denote the present evil age, for as long as it persists.
Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away.
“But about that day or hour no one knows, neither the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father.
Beware, keep alert; for you do not know when the time will come.
It is like a man going on a journey, when he leaves home and puts his slaves in charge, each with his work, and commands the doorkeeper to be on the watch.
34 : , like an absentee landlord, God will arrive to see what care the tenants have taken; see 12.1–11; Mt 24.45–51; 25.13–30; Lk 12.35–46; 19.11–27.
Therefore, keep awake—for you do not know when the master of the house will come, in the evening, or at midnight, or at cockcrow, or at dawn,
35 : , the four segments of the night watch.
or else he may find you asleep when he comes suddenly.
And what I say to you I say to all: Keep awake.”
8.27–15.47: Jesus's messianic mission: sacrifice.
11.1–15.47: Jesus's destiny in Jerusalem.
11.1–13.37: Jesus in Jerusalem, during Passover.
13.1–37: Jesus predicts the Temple's destruction and cataclysmic events to follow (Mt 24.1–44; Lk 21.5–33). Mark uses this teaching to interpret his audience's present reality (see esp. 13.14).
32–37 : Jesus rejects predicting specific timing but exhorts hearers to readiness; see v. 23; 14.32–42; Rom 13.11–14; 1 Thess 5:1–11.
37 : , Jesus's message extends beyond his immediate companions; see v. 14n.
